sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Constipation is infrequent, difficult, or uncomfortable passage of stool. Most cases are benign, but prehospital assessment should focus on excluding bowel obstruction, peritonitis, systemic illness, medication-related causes, or faecal impaction with complications.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Reduced stool frequency relative to the patient's usual pattern
- Hard stool, straining, bloating, or abdominal discomfort
- Nausea or reduced appetite
- Overflow diarrhoea, urinary retention, or rectal discomfort in faecal impaction
- Vomiting, severe pain, or marked distension in more concerning presentations

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Ask about opioid use, anticholinergics, iron, calcium channel blockers, antidepressants, and GLP-1 receptor agonists
- Red flags include severe or worsening abdominal pain, peritonism, persistent vomiting, marked distension, fever, inability to pass flatus, GI bleeding, or unexplained weight loss
- In older or frail patients, constipation may coexist with delirium, dehydration, urinary retention, or faecal impaction

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey and focused Vital Sign Survey
- Perform abdominal assessment and consider rectal history if relevant and within scope
- Encourage oral fluids if appropriate and advise mobilisation where safe
- Consider simple analgesia for discomfort
- Mild uncomplicated constipation may be managed with conservative advice or simple aperients within local practice, provided red flags are absent

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider antiemetic treatment if nausea or vomiting is significant
- If faecal impaction, obstruction, severe pain, dehydration, or systemic illness is suspected, escalate for medical review and transport rather than persisting with symptomatic treatment
- IV fluids may be appropriate when dehydration is clinically significant

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Provide advice on hydration, fibre, activity, and review of constipating medicines where appropriate
- Lack of response to simple measures or recurrent presentations warrants further medical review
